flashcards.front: What is an array in data structures?
flashcards.back: An array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations, allowing efficient random access using indices.

flashcards.front: What are the two main types of arrays?
flashcards.back: 1D Arrays (linear): store elements in a single row. 2D Arrays (matrix): store elements in rows and columns like a table.

flashcards.front: What is the index range in an array of n elements?
flashcards.back: In most programming languages, valid indices range from 0 to n-1 (zero-based indexing). Some languages like Fortran use 1-based indexing.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
flashcards.back: O(1) - constant time. This is the major advantage of arrays as any element can be accessed directly using its index.

flashcards.front: What is contiguous memory allocation in arrays?
flashcards.back: Array elements are stored in consecutive memory locations. This enables efficient random access but requires fixed memory allocation at declaration.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between static and dynamic arrays?
flashcards.back: Static arrays have fixed size declared at compile time. Dynamic arrays (like ArrayList in Java, list in Python) can resize during runtime to accommodate more elements.

flashcards.front: What is row-major order in 2D arrays?
flashcards.back: Elements are stored row by row - first complete row 0, then row 1, etc. Used in C, C++, Java, Python. Address calculation: Base + (i*n + j) * size.

flashcards.front: What is column-major order in 2D arrays?
flashcards.back: Elements are stored column by column - first complete column 0, then column 1, etc. Used in Fortran, MATLAB. Address calculation: Base + (i + j*m) * size.
A jagged array is a 2D array where each row can have a different number of columns. Also called array of arrays.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array?
flashcards.back: O(n) - linear time. All existing elements must be shifted one position to the right to make room for the new element.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of deleting an element from the end of an array?
flashcards.back: O(1) - constant time. No shifting required, just decrement the size (for dynamic arrays) or simply access the index.

flashcards.front: What are the main advantages of arrays?
flashcards.back: 1. O(1) random access. 2. Cache-friendly due to contiguous memory. 3. Simple implementation. 4. Memory efficient for same-type elements.

flashcards.front: What are the main disadvantages of arrays?
flashcards.back: 1. Fixed size (static arrays). 2. Expensive insertion/deletion in middle (O(n)). 3. Wastes memory if size overestimated. 4. Contiguous memory may not be available.

flashcards.front: What is base address in arrays?
flashcards.back: The base address is the memory location of the first element (index 0) of the array. All other element addresses are calculated relative to this base address.

flashcards.front: How do arrays differ from linked lists?
flashcards.back: Arrays provide O(1) access but O(n) insertion/deletion. Linked lists provide O(1) insertion/deletion but O(n) access. Arrays need contiguous memory; linked lists don't.
